reasoning: Càrn Dearg = Càrn (rocky hill) + Dearg (red) = red hill
Meall Dearg = Meall (lumpy hill) + Dearg (red) = red lumpy hill
Meall Garbh = Meall (lumpy hill) + Garbh (rough) = rough lumpy hill
Sgùrr Fiona = Sgùrr (peak) + Fionn/Fiona (white) = white peak
Sgùrr Mòr = Sgùrr (peak) + Mòr (big) = big peak
Creag Mhòr = Creag (rock) + Mhòr (big, lenited) = big rock
Garbh Bheinn = Garbh (rough) + Bheinn (mountain, lenited) = rough mountain
Fionn Bheinn = Fionn (white) + Bheinn (mountain, lenited) = white mountain
An Teallach = The Teallach (the forge) = the forge
Innis nan Damh = Innis (meadow) + nan (of the) + Damh (stags) = meadow of the stags
Càrn Gorm = Càrn (rocky hill) + Gorm (blue/green) = blue hill
Creag nan Damh = Creag (rock) + nan (of the) + Damh (stags) = rock of the stags
Beinn Teallach = Beinn (mountain) + Teallach (forge) = forge mountain
Meall Gorm = Meall (lumpy hill) + Gorm (blue/green) = green lumpy hill
Càrn Mòr Dearg = Càrn (rocky hill) + Mòr (big) + Dearg (red) = big red hill

The word 'Gorm' appears in both Càrn Gorm (blue hill) and Meall Gorm (green lumpy hill), corresponding to both 'blue' and 'green' in English. This is a common feature in Celtic languages where one word covers both blue and green.
